title: The Moon: reading informational text; Effects of the Moon
description: 2nd grade reading comprehension practice using informational text
topic_path: The Moon: reading informational text; Effects of the Moon

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage, then answer the practice question.

___

##Why Are There Tides?##
 
###Summer at the Beach###   
     
It's summer at the beach. You build the perfect sandcastle. Oh, no! Suddenly, a wave knocks it down. The water is getting closer and closer, so you grab your towel and run. But why does this happen?

![A diagram showing how the moon's gravitational pull creates high and low tides around the earth; high tides are created when the moon is orbiting near a body of water, and low tides are created when the moon is far away.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9d3434dc1139c8f71f0ce3d13068da02.jpg)
*Picture 1: the moon’s gravity pulls the water towards it, creating high tides.*

###Tides###

The tide, or ocean level, goes up and down every day. High tides and low tides are caused by the moon. The moon's gravity pulls things towards it. This pull makes the earth's water [[☃ definition 1]] or push out. This happens on the side that is closest to the moon, and on the side farthest from the moon too. These bulges of water are high tides.

As the earth spins around, your part of the earth passes through both of these bulges each day. When you're in one of the bulges, you get a high tide. When you're not in one of the bulges, you get a low tide. This cycle of two high tides and two low tides continues almost every day on most of the coastlines of the world.

![The same shoreline shown at high tide, with the water's edge near the camera, and low tide, with the water's edge far away in the distance.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6ed0f664e58ccdd35f1ec72d13d670ae.jpg)
*Picture 2: the same shoreline at high tide (left) and at low tide (right)*


[[☃ explanation 1]]

___

##Practice question##

Read the sentence, then select the correct word from the dropdown menu.

**The word [[☃ dropdown 2]] means “ocean level”.**

options.definition: a swelling, or a place where something gets bigger.

options.explanation:
Estimated 410-600L

*Text source: "Why Are There Tides?" is adapted from [“What Causes Tides?”](https://scijinks.gov/tides/) by SciJinks, which is in the [public domain](https://creativecommons.org/publicdomain/mark/1.0/).*

*Image source: [“Untitled”](https://scijinks.gov/tides/) by Samuel Wantman is licensed under [CC BY-SA 3.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/)*

##Why Are There Tides?##
 
###Summer at the Beach###   
     
It's summer at the beach. You build the perfect sandcastle. Oh, no! Suddenly, a wave knocks it down. The water is getting closer and closer, so you grab your towel and run. But why does this happen?

![A diagram showing how the moon's gravitational pull creates high and low tides around the earth; high tides are created when the moon is orbiting near a body of water, and low tides are created when the moon is far away.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9d3434dc1139c8f71f0ce3d13068da02.jpg)
*Picture 1: the moon’s gravity pulls the water towards it, creating high tides.*

###Tides###

The tide, or ocean level, goes up and down every day. High tides and low tides are caused by the moon. The moon's __gravity__ pulls things towards it. This pull makes the earth's water [[☃ definition 2]] or push out. This happens on the side that is closest to the moon, and on the side farthest from the moon too. These bulges of water are high tides.

As the earth spins around, your part of the earth passes through both of these bulges each day. When you're in one of the bulges, you get a high tide. When you're not in one of the bulges, you get a low tide. This cycle of two high tides and two low tides continues almost every day on most of the coastlines of the world.

![The same shoreline shown at high tide, with the water's edge near the camera, and low tide, with the water's edge far away in the distance.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6ed0f664e58ccdd35f1ec72d13d670ae.jpg)
*Picture 2: the same shoreline at high tide (left) and at low tide (right)*


[[☃ explanation 2]]

___

##Practice question##

Read this part of the passage.

“High tides and low tides are caused by the moon. The moon's __gravity__ pulls things towards it. This pull makes the Earth's water bulge or push out.”

**What words in the sentences best help you understand the meaning of the word __gravity__?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: A bulge is a swelling or a place where something gets bigger.

options.explanation:
Estimated 410-600L

##Why Are There Tides?##
 
###Summer at the Beach###   
     
It's summer at the beach. You build the perfect sandcastle. Oh, no! Suddenly, a wave knocks it down. The water is getting closer and closer, so you grab your towel and run. But why does this happen?

![A diagram showing how the moon's gravitational pull creates high and low tides around the earth; high tides are created when the moon is orbiting near a body of water, and low tides are created when the moon is far away.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9d3434dc1139c8f71f0ce3d13068da02.jpg)
*Picture 1: the moon’s gravity pulls the water towards it, creating high tides.*

###Tides###

The tide, or ocean level, goes up and down every day. High tides and low tides are caused by the moon. The moon's gravity pulls things towards it. This pull makes the earth's water [[☃ definition 2]] or push out. This happens on the side that is closest to the moon, and on the side farthest from the moon too. These bulges of water are high tides.

As the earth spins around, your part of the earth passes through both of these bulges each day. When you're in one of the bulges, you get a high tide. When you're not in one of the bulges, you get a low tide. This cycle of two high tides and two low tides __continues__ almost every day on most of the coastlines of the world.

![The same shoreline shown at high tide, with the water's edge near the camera, and low tide, with the water's edge far away in the distance.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6ed0f664e58ccdd35f1ec72d13d670ae.jpg)

##The Beautiful Mysterious Moon##

![Carlos reading a book and the poem's speaker smiling while the moon shines in the background.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/3bff695453cf0a28e91008ba6f95726f.png)

I thought there was a rabbit inside the moon,   
One that nibbled on lettuce and mushrooms.   
But Carlos says that those are just craters.   
 
*That’s the way the moon works, the beautiful mysterious moon.*
 
I thought there was a giant in the sea   
That made the waves and pushed them onto the beach.   
But Carlos says that those are just tides.   
 
*That’s the way the moon works, the beautiful mysterious moon.*
 
I thought there was an old lady under the underground  
With a giant magnet that held us in place when we walk around.   
But Carlos says that it’s just gravity.   

*That’s the way the moon works, the beautiful mysterious moon.*

I don’t know if I agree or disagree,   
That the moon is responsible for tides and gravity.   
But Carlos read the science book, so I’ll have to believe   
That thing in the sky has an effect on you and me.   

[[☃ explanation 2]]

___

When you're not in one of the bulges, you get a low tide. This cycle of two high tides and two low tides continues almost every day on most of the coastlines of the world.

![The same shoreline shown at high tide, with the water's edge near the camera, and low tide, with the water's edge far away in the distance.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6ed0f664e58ccdd35f1ec72d13d670ae.jpg)
*Picture 2: the same shoreline at high tide (left) and at low tide (right)*


[[☃ explanation 3]]

___

##The Beautiful Mysterious Moon##

![Carlos reading a book and the poem's speaker smiling while the moon shines in the background.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/3bff695453cf0a28e91008ba6f95726f.png)

I thought there was a rabbit inside the moon,   
One that nibbled on lettuce and mushrooms.   
But Carlos says that those are just craters.   
 
*That’s the way the moon works, the beautiful mysterious moon.*
 
I thought there was a giant in the sea   
That made the waves and pushed them onto the beach.   
But Carlos says that those are just tides.   
 
*That’s the way the moon works, the beautiful mysterious moon.*
 
I thought there was an old lady under the underground  
With a giant magnet that held us in place when we walk around.   
But Carlos says that it’s just gravity.   

*That’s the way the moon works, the beautiful mysterious moon.*

I don’t know if I agree or disagree,   
That the moon is responsible for tides and gravity.   
But Carlos read the science book, so I’ll have to believe   
That thing in the sky has an effect on you and me.   

[[☃ explanation 2]]

___

##Practice question##

**Which lines from the poem are the opposite of the information in the passage “Why Are There Tides?”**

[[☃ radio 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage and the poem, then answer the practice question.

___

##Why Are There Tides?##
 
###Summer at the Beach###   
     
It's summer at the beach. You build the perfect sandcastle. Oh, no! Suddenly, a wave knocks it down. The water is getting closer and closer, so you grab your towel and run. But why does this happen?

![A diagram showing how the moon's gravitational pull creates high and low tides around the earth; high tides are created when the moon is orbiting near a body of water, and low tides are created when the moon is far away.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9d3434dc1139c8f71f0ce3d13068da02.jpg)
*Picture 1: the moon’s gravity pulls the water towards it, creating high tides.*

###Tides###

The tide, or ocean level, goes up and down every day. High tides and low tides are caused by the moon. The moon's gravity pulls things towards it. This pull makes the earth's water [[☃ definition 2]] or push out. This happens on the side that is closest to the moon, and on the side farthest from the moon too. These bulges of water are high tides.

As the earth spins around, your part of the earth passes through both of these bulges each day. When you're in one of the bulges, you get a high tide. When you're not in one of the bulges, you get a low tide. This cycle of two high tides and two low tides continues almost every day on most of the coastlines of the world.

![The same shoreline shown at high tide, with the water's edge near the camera, and low tide, with the water's edge far away in the distance.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6ed0f664e58ccdd35f1ec72d13d670ae.jpg)
*Picture 2: the same shoreline at high tide (left) and at low tide (right)*


[[☃ explanation 3]]

___

I thought there was a rabbit inside the moon,   
One that nibbled on lettuce and mushrooms.   
But Carlos says that those are just craters.   
 
*That’s the way the moon works, the beautiful mysterious moon.*
 
I thought there was a giant in the sea   
That made the waves and pushed them onto the beach.   
But Carlos says that those are just tides.   
 
*That’s the way the moon works, the beautiful mysterious moon.*
 
I thought there was an old lady under the underground  
With a giant magnet that held us in place when we walk around.   
But Carlos says that it’s just gravity.   

*That’s the way the moon works, the beautiful mysterious moon.*

I don’t know if I agree or disagree,   
That the moon is responsible for tides and gravity.   
But Carlos read the science book, so I’ll have to believe   
That thing in the sky has an effect on you and me.
